reviews.summary: This paper proposes MemPrune, a neural network pruning method using per-weight gradient dispersion (GDS) as a criterion to identify memorization-contributing weights. The core hypothesis—that high gradient dispersion indicates memorization—is tested via canary validation and found to be unsupported (ρ=-0.026). The paper presents this negative result honestly but has serious data reporting issues in Table 1 that undermine its credibility.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I conducted extensive online searches for related work. Key findings: (1) Agarwal et al. (2022) 'Variance of Gradients' (VoG) already proposed using gradient variance to identify memorized examples—MemPrune simply adapts this from sample-level (across epochs) to weight-level (at one checkpoint). (2) Most critically, 'Controllable Memorization in LLMs via Weight Pruning' (EMNLP 2025) uses nearly identical gradient-based weight pruning for memorization control in LLMs. The authors fail to cite this highly relevant concurrent work. The paper's main contribution is actually a negative result (GDS doesn't capture memorization), which has some value but limited novelty given the strong related work.
strengths: The paper honestly reports a negative result (canary validation shows ρ=-0.026, refuting the core hypothesis), which is scientifically valuable
The writing is generally clear and the paper is well-organized
The authors provide comprehensive experimental logs and release code/results
The hybrid criterion (GDS + magnitude) ablation provides a practical, usable configuration
The paper connects to relevant theory (Coherent Gradients Hypothesis)
weaknesses: CRITICAL: Table 1 reports MemPrune at 90% sparsity as '48.25 ± 32.33%' but results.json shows ALL seeds at 90% have ~10% accuracy (random chance). This appears to be data fabrication or severe misreporting.
CRITICAL: At 70% sparsity, only 1 of 3 seeds works (seed 43: 89.91%, seeds 42/44: 10%), yet the paper reports '90.20 ± 0.85%' as if all seeds performed similarly.
The DP-SGD combination experiments (a key part of the proposal) all failed with errors but this is buried in limitations rather than disclosed upfront.
The paper fails to cite 'Controllable Memorization in LLMs via Weight Pruning' (EMNLP 2025), which uses very similar gradient-based weight pruning for memorization control.
The privacy 'improvements' at 90% sparsity are meaningless—the model has collapsed to random performance (MIA AUC ~0.5 because the model has lost all learned representations).
The theoretical motivation (Coherent Gradients Hypothesis) is experimentally refuted by the paper's own canary validation, leaving the method without theoretical grounding.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper's core premise—that gradient dispersion captures memorization—is experimentally refuted by your own canary validation (ρ=-0.026). This is an important negative result, but the paper tries to salvage a 'method' from this failed hypothesis. More seriously, there are major data integrity issues: Table 1 reports accuracy at 90% sparsity as 48.25±32.33%, but the results.json shows ALL seeds at 90% achieved only ~10% accuracy (random chance on CIFAR-10). Similarly, at 70% sparsity, only 1 of 3 seeds worked (seed 43: 89.91%, seeds 42/44: 10%), yet you report a mean of 90.20±0.85%—this is cherry-picking. The high variance (32.33%) at 90% suggests you're including some failed runs and some that didn't, but the raw data shows universal failure. You must: (1) Verify and correct all numbers in Table 1, (2) Report the actual catastrophic failure rate across seeds, (3) Acknowledge that 'privacy gains' at high sparsity are artifacts of model collapse, (4) Cite and discuss the EMNLP 2025 paper on controllable memorization via gradient-based pruning, which makes your contribution significantly less novel.
questions_for_authors: Why does Table 1 report 48.25±32.33% accuracy at 90% sparsity when results.json shows ALL seeds at 90% achieved ~10% accuracy?
Why does Table 1 report 90.20±0.85% at 70% sparsity when only 1 of 3 seeds worked (seed 43: 89.91%, seeds 42/44: 10%)?
How do you justify claiming MIA AUC reduction as a privacy 'improvement' when the model has collapsed to random performance?
Why was the EMNLP 2025 paper 'Controllable Memorization in LLMs via Weight Pruning' not cited, despite using very similar gradient-based weight pruning for memorization control?
reviews.integrity_check: MAJOR ISSUES IDENTIFIED: (1) Table 1 data does NOT match results.json—the reported accuracy at 90% sparsity (48.25%) appears fabricated or severely misreported; actual results show 10% accuracy across all seeds. (2) At 70% sparsity, the paper reports 90.20±0.85% but results.json shows 2 of 3 seeds collapsed to 10% accuracy—only seed 43 worked. This is cherry-picking. (3) DP-SGD experiments all failed with Opacus errors. (4) The 'privacy improvement' at 90% sparsity is meaningless—the MIA AUC is ~0.5 because the model outputs are random, not because privacy is protected. These are not minor discrepancies; they indicate the paper's quantitative claims are unreliable.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes MemPrune, a pruning criterion based on per-weight gradient dispersion, with the goal of removing memorization-heavy weights and reducing membership inference attack vulnerability. Empirically, it reports modest privacy gains at moderate sparsity, a severe collapse at high sparsity, and an important negative finding from a canary experiment: the proposed score does not correlate with memorization. The paper is clearly written and the negative-result framing is more honest than many submissions, but the experiment artifacts are internally inconsistent with the reported tables.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required novelty checks using exact-title searches, technique-name searches, component-combination searches, searches around the cited pruning/privacy baselines, and searches of recent venue/proceedings pages. I did not find a prior paper proposing exactly this combination of per-weight gradient-dispersion pruning for privacy; the closest lines are pruning+MIA papers such as Yuan & Zhang 2022 (USENIX), Shang et al. 2025 (NDSS), pruning-as-privacy work such as Huang et al. 2020 (arXiv), sample-level gradient-variance work such as Agarwal et al. 2022 (CVPR VoG), and variance-based pruning such as Berisha et al. 2025 (ICCV). So the idea is not obviously duplicated, but it is a fairly incremental recombination of existing ingredients rather than a strongly new conceptual advance. Example sources checked online include https://www.usenix.org/conference/usenixsecurity22/presentation/yuan-xiaoyong , https://dev.ndss-symposium.org/wp-content/uploads/2025-90-paper.pdf , https://arxiv.org/abs/2003.01876 , https://openreview.net/forum?id=ryeFY0EFwS , https://arxiv.org/abs/2003.07422 , https://proceedings.mlr.press/v119/harutyunyan20a.html , https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.03262 , and https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/ICCV2025/html/Berisha_Variance-Based_Pruning_for_Accelerating_and_Compressing_Trained_Networks_ICCV_2025_paper.html .
strengths: The paper asks a meaningful question at the intersection of pruning and privacy rather than optimizing accuracy alone.
The canary experiment is scientifically valuable: the main theoretical hypothesis is explicitly tested and the negative result is reported rather than hidden.
There is a reasonable set of privacy baselines and attack metrics, including LiRA, loss-based MIA, and confidence-based MIA.
The paper is generally well organized, and the main takeaways are easy to follow.
Code, logs, and multiple result files are present, which is better than many submissions.
weaknesses: The paper fails the required integrity check: multiple numbers in the paper do not match `results.json`. For example, the paper reports MemPrune at 70% sparsity as 90.20 ± 0.85 accuracy and 0.563 ± 0.004 loss-AUC, while `results.json` gives 36.64 ± 37.67 and 0.519 ± 0.027 from seeds [10.0, 89.91, 10.0].
The mismatch is even more severe at 90% and 95% sparsity: the paper reports 48.25 ± 32.33 and 27.69 ± 25.01 accuracy for MemPrune, but `results.json` records 10.00 ± 0.00 at both sparsities.

The LiRA table is inconsistent with the raw artifact: the paper claims 8 shadow models and TPR@1%FPR values 0.018/0.014/0.009, while top-level `results.json` stores 4 shadow models with 0.002/0.006/0.004. There is also disagreement between `results.json` and `exp/lira_evaluation/results.json`.

Figure provenance is not trustworthy: `generate_figures.py` expects a schema (`dense_baselines`, `summary`, nested ablation `mia` fields) that does not match the current `results.json`, so I cannot verify that the shipped figures were generated from the current raw results.
The core scientific claim is weakened by the canary result: the proposed pruning score does not measure memorization, so the theoretical motivation is largely refuted by the paper's own evidence.

Experimental rigor is limited beyond CIFAR-10/ResNet-18: generalization experiments are single-seed, random pruning is missing in some cross-domain comparisons, and the LiRA setup is much smaller than standard practice.

There are multiple competing pipelines (`run_experiments.py`, `run_continuation.py`, `run_experiments_v2.py`, intermediate JSONs, summary markdown) producing conflicting artifacts, which substantially hurts reproducibility.
reviews.detailed_feedback: If the authors want this work to be reviewable, the first priority is not more experiments but artifact reconciliation. They need to declare one authoritative pipeline, regenerate `results.json` from that pipeline, and ensure every paper table and figure is programmatically derived from that exact file. Right now the workspace contains mutually inconsistent versions of the same results (`results.json`, `exp/*/results.json`, `RESULTS_SUMMARY.md`, and the experiment logs), and this alone forces rejection. After fixing integrity, the paper would still need a tighter scientific framing: the contribution is better described as an empirical study of a plausible but ultimately unsupported pruning signal than as a new memorization-aware method. I would suggest centering the negative result, explicitly comparing against simpler capacity-control explanations, adding multi-seed validation for the canary and generalization experiments, including the missing random baseline everywhere, and re-running LiRA with a clearly documented and consistent setup.
questions_for_authors: Which artifact is authoritative for review: top-level `results.json`, `exp/*/results.json`, `RESULTS_SUMMARY.md`, or the continuation logs?
Why do the main MemPrune numbers in the paper differ substantially from top-level `results.json`, especially at 70%, 90%, and 95% sparsity?
Why does the paper state 8-shadow-model LiRA while top-level `results.json` records 4 shadow models and different TPR values?
Can you provide a script that deterministically regenerates every table and figure in the paper from one canonical results file?
Are the paper's 70-95% MemPrune results coming from `memprune_global`, `memprune_proportional`, or a different code path entirely?
reviews.integrity_check: Source code and logs are present and the logs do show real training activity, but the paper does not agree with the declared raw artifact. Several table values differ from `results.json`, LiRA metadata is inconsistent across files, and the figure-generation script does not match the current result schema. Because the reported numbers are not uniquely traceable to a single consistent raw result file, I do not consider the empirical evidence trustworthy in its current form.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes MemPrune, which uses per-weight gradient dispersion (variance of per-sample gradients) as a pruning criterion to selectively remove memorization-contributing weights, aiming to simultaneously compress neural networks and reduce membership inference attack vulnerability. The key finding is a negative result: canary validation shows GDS does not correlate with memorization (ρ=-0.026), though GDS-based pruning provides modest MIA reduction at moderate sparsity (50-70%) through a mechanism different from the hypothesized memorization removal.
reviews.novelty_assessment: The core idea of using per-weight gradient variance as a memorization-aware pruning criterion is moderately novel. No prior work proposes exactly this combination. However, the individual components are well-established: VoG (Agarwal et al., CVPR 2022) uses gradient variance for sample-level memorization estimation; Yuan et al. (USENIX Security 2022) and WeMem (Shang et al., NDSS 2025) study pruning-privacy connections; Berisha et al. (ICCV 2025) propose variance-based structured pruning. The paper's key contribution of transferring VoG from the sample domain to the weight domain is a natural extension, and since the paper's own canary validation shows this transfer does not work (ρ=-0.026), the novelty of the positive method is undercut by its own negative finding. The negative result itself is interesting but not surprising given the fundamental difference between temporal per-sample variance and spatial per-weight variance, which the paper acknowledges.
strengths: Honest reporting of negative results: the paper transparently presents the canary validation showing GDS does not capture memorization, which is commendable and rare in ML research
Well-structured experimental framework with multiple MIA attack types (loss-based, confidence-based, LiRA), multiple baselines (random, magnitude, gradient sensitivity), and sparsity sweeps
Clear theoretical motivation from the Coherent Gradients Hypothesis, with a well-formulated method (GDS computation via Welford's algorithm with layer-wise normalization)
The hybrid criterion (α=0.1) is a practical contribution showing how to incorporate GDS signal into standard magnitude pruning
Good related work coverage positioning the contribution relative to VoG, WeMem, and other privacy-pruning literature
weaknesses: CRITICAL: Major discrepancies between paper tables and results.json. MemPrune at 70% sparsity: paper claims 90.20±0.85% accuracy, results.json shows 36.64±37.67% (2 of 3 seeds collapsed to 10%). At 90%: paper claims 48.25±32.33%, results.json shows 10.00±0.00%. At 95%: paper claims 27.69±25.01%, results.json shows 10.00±0.00%. These are not minor rounding differences—they represent fundamentally different experimental outcomes.

LiRA evaluation discrepancy: paper states '8 shadow models' but results.json metadata says 4 shadow models. TPR@1%FPR for dense: paper claims 0.018 vs results.json 0.002. These numbers cannot both be correct.

The acknowledged ablation code path inconsistency (45.69% vs ~10% for the same configuration) raises concerns about whether the main table also used inconsistent code paths to obtain more favorable results.

The core hypothesis is definitively refuted by the paper's own experiments (ρ=-0.026), making the method's theoretical foundation invalid. The observed privacy benefits are attributed to generic capacity reduction, which random pruning also achieves (and at high sparsity, random pruning provides better privacy-accuracy tradeoffs).

DP-SGD experiments, described as a 'primary success criterion' from the proposal, were not completed. ImageNet-100 experiments were omitted. Random pruning baselines missing from generalization table. Single-seed canary validation. These are substantial gaps.

At 50% sparsity (the best operating point), MemPrune's privacy improvement is 2.6pp MIA AUC reduction with 1.25pp accuracy loss. This is a modest practical gain, and the paper does not demonstrate it is statistically significant.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper investigates an interesting question—whether per-weight gradient variance can identify memorization-contributing weights for privacy-aware pruning—and arrives at an honest negative result. However, the paper cannot be accepted in its current form due to severe results integrity issues. The numbers reported in the main table (Table 1) do not match results.json for MemPrune at 70%, 90%, and 95% sparsity. For example, at 70% sparsity, the paper reports 90.20% accuracy suggesting the method works at moderate sparsity, but results.json shows that 2 out of 3 seeds catastrophically collapsed to 10%, with only one seed (43) achieving 89.91%. The paper's reported mean of 90.20 is impossible given these raw results. Similarly, at 90% all three seeds show exactly 10.00% accuracy in results.json, but the paper reports 48.25±32.33%. The LiRA evaluation also has discrepancies (8 vs 4 shadow models, different TPR values). These issues must be resolved before the paper can be evaluated on its scientific merits. If revised with accurate numbers, the paper would need to honestly acknowledge that MemPrune only reliably works at 50% sparsity (and inconsistently at 70%), which significantly weakens the contribution. The negative canary result is the most valuable finding and could be strengthened with multi-seed validation. The hybrid criterion merits further investigation at moderate sparsity levels where pure GDS pruning is viable.
questions_for_authors: The paper reports MemPrune accuracy of 90.20±0.85% at 70% sparsity, but results.json shows 36.64±37.67% (seeds 42,44=10%, seed43=89.91%). How was the paper's number computed? Were different experimental runs used?
At 90% sparsity, results.json shows 10.00% accuracy for all three seeds, yet the paper reports 48.25±32.33%. Where do these numbers come from?
The paper states '8 shadow models' for LiRA but results.json metadata says 4. Which is correct?
Given that 2 out of 3 seeds fail at 70% sparsity, is MemPrune's success at moderate sparsity seed-dependent? What determines whether the method collapses?
Since random pruning achieves better privacy-accuracy tradeoffs at high sparsity, what is the practical advantage of MemPrune over random pruning at the moderate sparsity levels where it works?
reviews.integrity_check: FAILED. Multiple critical discrepancies found between paper tables and results.json: (1) MemPrune accuracy at 70% sparsity: paper 90.20% vs results.json 36.64%; (2) MemPrune accuracy at 90%: paper 48.25% vs results.json 10.00%; (3) MemPrune accuracy at 95%: paper 27.69% vs results.json 10.00%; (4) LiRA shadow model count: paper says 8, results.json says 4; (5) LiRA TPR values differ substantially. Experiment source code exists and implements what the paper describes. The code for GDS computation (Welford's algorithm, per-sample backward passes, layer-wise normalization) and MIA evaluation (loss-based, confidence-based, LiRA) is correct and well-implemented. However, the paper appears to report results from a different or modified experimental run than what is recorded in results.json, which is a serious integrity concern.

(2) At 70% sparsity, the paper reports 90.20±0.85% but results.json shows 2 of 3 seeds collapsed to 10% accuracy—only seed 43 worked. This is cherry-picking. Right now the workspace contains mutually inconsistent versions of the same results (`results.json`, `exp/*/results.json`, `RESULTS_SUMMARY.md`, and the experiment logs), and this alone forces rejection. After fixing integrity, the paper would still need a tighter scientific framing: the contribution is better described as an empirical study of a plausible but ultimately unsupported pruning signal than as a new memorization-aware method. I would suggest centering the negative result, explicitly comparing against simpler capacity-control explanations, adding multi-seed validation for the canary and generalization experiments, including the missing random baseline everywhere, and re-running LiRA with a clearly documented and consistent setup.

Several table values differ from `results.json`, LiRA metadata is inconsistent across files, and the figure-generation script does not match the current result schema. Because the reported numbers are not uniquely traceable to a single consistent raw result file, I do not consider the empirical evidence trustworthy in its current form.
